_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 8-21 Violent, Chemical, and Hazardous Scene (Revised 12/27/2022, MDHHS Reviewed 2022). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan System Protocol Section 8-21: Violent, Chemical, and Hazardous Scene — MDHHS Approved 12/27/2022
purpose: Govern EMS provider safety protocols for violent, chemical, and hazardous scenes. EMS provider safety is the first priority — providers do not enter unsafe scenes. EMS providers are not trained or equipped for law enforcement or hazmat entry operations. Stage at a safe distance and wait for scene clearance before entering.

sections.title: Scene Safety — General Principles
points: Scene safety assessment begins with dispatch information and continues throughout the call
EMS providers are NOT required to enter scenes that pose an immediate threat to their safety
Stage at a safe distance and request law enforcement or hazmat to clear/secure the scene before EMS entry
Scene safety is a dynamic assessment — a scene that appears safe may become unsafe; be prepared to retreat
If a scene becomes unsafe after EMS arrival: retreat immediately; do not attempt to continue patient care in an unsafe environment

sections.title: Violent Scene
points: DO NOT enter: active shooting, active fight/assault, uncontrolled violent suspect on scene
Stage at safe distance (minimum one block or out of visual line); await law enforcement scene clearance
Active Threat / Warm Zone operations: only if specifically trained and authorized for tactical EMS (TEMS); standard EMS is NOT trained or equipped for warm zone operations
After scene is cleared by law enforcement: enter and provide care
Restrained patients: law enforcement must accompany the patient per Section 7-17 (Patient Restraint)
Crime scene: preserve evidence per Section 7-3 (Crime Scene Management)

sections.title: Hazardous Materials Scene
points: DO NOT enter the hazmat hot zone or warm zone without appropriate PPE and hazmat training
Stage in the cold zone — uphill, upwind, upstream from the hazmat incident
Hazmat-trained teams must perform patient decontamination before EMS patient care
EMS provides medical support in the cold zone after decontamination
Contaminated patients entering an ambulance can incapacitate the crew — ensure decontamination is complete
Contact poison control or CHEMTREC for hazardous substance exposure guidance: 1-800-424-9300

sections.title: Chemical/Nerve Agent Exposure
points: SLUDGE/SLUDGEM symptoms (Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis, Miosis): suspect organophosphate or nerve agent exposure
Follow nerve agent/organophosphate protocols if available and trained
Decontaminate patient thoroughly before providing care
Self-protection: do NOT become a secondary victim — stay out of contamination zone until hazmat clears scene

deterioration_flags: Scene becomes unsafe after EMS arrival — retreat immediately; do not delay for patient care; request law enforcement
SLUDGE/SLUDGEM symptoms in multiple patients — suspect nerve agent/organophosphate mass casualty; stage upwind; notify hazmat and public health
scope_notes: Scene safety FIRST — do not enter unsafe scene
Violent scene: stage at safe distance; await law enforcement clearance
Active threat/warm zone: only TEMS-trained providers; standard EMS does NOT enter warm zone
Hazmat: cold zone only after decontamination; uphill, upwind, upstream
Contaminated patients must be decontaminated before entering ambulance
SLUDGE/SLUDGEM: nerve agent/organophosphate; decontaminate; poison control 1-800-424-9300
Self-protection: do NOT become a secondary victim
